
---
title: "Documentation for imfcharts"
date: "today"
author:
  - name: Datatools Team
    email: datatools-support@imf.org
    url: https://intlmonetaryfund.sharepoint.com/sites/fw-datatools
    department: 
      - name: ITD
      - url: http://datatools/
bibliography: references.bib
number-sections: true
code-block-bg: "#F0F0F0"
format:
  html:
    # styles.css is needed to scale down figure sizes.
    # Specifying any of
    # #| out-width: "25%"
    # #| fig-width: 8
    # #| fig-height: 5
    # for individual figures does not have any effect.
    css: styles.css
    link-external-newwindow: true
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-float: true
    toc-location: left
    theme: custom-theme.scss # cyborg
    grid:
      body-width: 1200px  # Main body width
      margin-width: 150px # width of toc
      sidebar-width: 300px # left side
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    execute:
      echo: false

#  pdf:
#    documentclass: report
#    papersize: letter

# Adjust title font
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <style>
        .title {
          font-size: 48px; /* Adjust the pixel value as needed */
          color: #4B82AD;    /* Optional: change the color */
        }
      </style>
jupyter: python3
---

<!-- 2025-11-20                                             -->
<!--                                                        -->
<!-- Documentation on imfcharts.                            -->
<!-- To generate this document run                          -->
<!-- quarto render imfcharts.qmd                            -->
<!--                                                        -->
<!-- Geneating PDF output will open a Carbon Black window   -->
<!-- but ignoring it will render the document.              -->

```{python}
#| label: py-imports
import os
import sys

# Create data dir
if not os.path.isdir('data'):
    os.makedirs('data')

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib

# Tables in quarto
from itables import show
from IPython.display import Markdown

import imf_datatools

from imfcharts import *
set_style('imf-articleiv')
```

<!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------>
# Introduction {#sec-intro}
This is the documentation for the `imfcharts` package.
The goal of this package is simple, to allow easy (re)production of IMF charts for publications.
The technology to do this has been available for decades, our goal is to make this as simple as it should be.
Furthermore, the technology we utilize is open source, meaning that the technology will be available for use
independent of any commercial entities. This will become increasingly important in the age of AI, as this provides
the Fund with a way of integrating with such systems but at the same time retaining the core technology in-house
so that we are not locked into one platform.

The authors wish to thank the [Process Modernization Challenge](https://imf.brightidea.com/Process){target="_blank"}
committee for their support and encouragement. The project started out as an [idea](https://imf.brightidea.com/D1580){target="_blank"}
for the Challenge, and in Feb 2025 the project [received First Place in the Challenge event](https://imf.brightidea.com/Process){target="_blank"}.

This document is orgnaized as follows.
In the next subsections @sec-quickstart is a Quick Start on how to install,
followed by  @sec-motivation Motivation, and examples are provided in @sec-gallery.
The following sections show the basics of chart creation in  @sec-basics, followed by how to switch styles in @sec-styles,
and integration with other systems are shown in @sec-R for R, @sec-stata for Stata and @sec-ai for AI.

## Quick Start and Installation {#sec-quickstart}

The library is written in the Python language, but can be called from R (and perhaps in the future) Stata.
In all cases the Python library must be installed. This library is based on the well-known open source [Matplotlib](https://matplotlib.org/){target="_blank"}
library which is the basis for most Python visualization libraries.
Beyond Matplotlib the only dependencies are `numpy` and `pandas` so the library should be usable in any environment.

To install the library,

1. Install Python from the Software Center

2. Copy the folder `\\ecnswn12p\ems_shared\pub\datatools\dev\imfcharts` to your computer's `C:\ProgramData\Python3\Lib\site-packages` folder

3. Open Command Prompt and run the following:
    a. `ipython` Open ipython session
    b. `from imfcharts import *` If no error message appears the installation is complete

Note that importing the library should create a copy of style files in your computer's `C:\User\[username]\.matplotlib\stylelib` folder so they can be accessed by `Matplotlib`.

To create a chart, prepare a `pandas.DataFrame` with your data and set the index to be what you want your x-axis to be.
For example, in most cases a time series is plotted, so the index should be set to the dates.

A simple example is given below.

```{python}
#| label: quickstart
#| code-fold: show
#| fig-cap: "Simple GDP plot."

import pandas as pd
from imfcharts import *

# Create data
data = {'dates'                   : ['2024Q1', '2024Q2', '2024Q3', '2024Q4', '2025Q1'],
        'Consumption'             : [2.5, 3, 2.8, 2.7, 1.3],
        'Gov'                     : [1.2, 1.1, 0.8, 0.3, -0.2],
        'Net exports'             : [-0.5, 0.3, 1.2, 0.3, -1.5],
        'Fixed capital formation' : [0.2, 0.3, -0.2, -0.3, -0.3]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data).set_index('dates')
df['GDP'] = df.sum(axis=1)

# Get all columns except "GDP" for bars
barcols = list(df.columns)
barcols.remove('GDP')

# Create chart
chart = Chart(df, linecols='GDP', barcols=barcols)

# Show chart
chart
```

As can be seen, the code needed to generate the chart is the following single line:

```{python}
#| label: quickstart-chart
#| code-fold: show
#| eval: false
chart = Chart(df, linecols='GDP', barcols=barcols)
```

The chart automatically uses the default IMF Article IV colors, which is set by the library as the default colors.
Adding a few options will make the chart more presentable:

```{python}
#| label: quickstart-better
#| code-fold: show
#| fig-cap: "Modified GDP plot."

chart = Chart(df, linecols='GDP', barcols=barcols,
              attrs={'GDP' : {'color' : 'black'}},
              title='GDP by Composition', subtitle='(Seasonally Adjusted)',
              yrange=[-3, 5],
              legend_bottom=0.1, ncol_legend=2)
chart
```

The differences with the first chart are:

- Added title, subtitle
- Specifid line for "GDP" to be black
- Specified y-axis range
- Moved legend bottom, specified number of columns in legend

All of these options and more are explained in @sec-basics.
The `chart` object can be saved with the command:

```{python}
#| label: quickstart-save
#| code-fold: show
#| eval: false

chart.save('GDP.pdf') # change extention to png etc.
```

and can be shown on screen with the command:

```{python}
#| label: quickstart-show
#| code-fold: show
#| eval: false

chart.show()
```

The `chart` variable is a `Chart` object within the library, and provides a direct interface with the underlying
`matplotlib.pyplot.Figure` (`chart.fig`) and `matplotlib.pyplot.Axes` (`chart.ax`) objects so that they can be modified further.
For example,  the popular [`seaborn`](https://seaborn.pydata.org/){target="_blank"} library is built on top of `Matplotlib`
so it can be called on the created `Chart`.

<!-- TODO: Add example -->

## Motivation {#sec-motivation}

Un until now the Fund has wasted enormous resources and time by not provding a standard program to generate charts[^chart-programs],
and this has hurt the Fund in multiple ways:

1. People (especially young ones) are forced to repeat menial tasks ad nauseam
2. The mindset is stuck in Excel and people do not understand that technology can reduce these efforts significantly
3. The standards of visualization are stuck with outdated standards and terrible aesthetics

[^chart-programs]: The ITD CATS team provides the Excel PCF tool for this purpose, but this is still contained within Excel. Furthermore, it has not been updated for many years.

Therefore the goals of this project are:

1. Provide a package to easily generate existing IMF charts
2. Provide templates that allow users to easily switch to a new style

This allows Fund users (especially young ones) to realize that menial tasks can be automated and done with much less effort.
Once a standard library is available and widely used, code written using it can then be reused.
It also incentivizes the Fund to move to better standards of visualization since the transition is now much easier and
does not require editing every single Excel chart that has been created previously.
The `imfcharts` library currently provides a "classic" IMF Article IV style (`imf-articleiv.mplstyle`)
but this can easily be expanded to other styles such as for WEO, GFSR, REO, etc.

While the main library we provide is written in Python, it can also be called from R using a simple syntax transformation (in the future possibly from Stata),
which expands the user base.
Given recent advances in AI systems, it is also possible to use these tools through AI, so that even without any knowledge of Python or R,
users can use the library through generative AI tools.
The crucial point with AI is that although the AI system can be the one calling the library, the library itself is maintained in-house and
the Fund retains full control of it. The use of open source, particularly Python ensures that any AI system can use it.
See @sec-ai on further details of usage with AI systems.

Finally, for users of Python and R, combining this library with the [datatools](http://datatools){target="_blank"} provides an end-to-end solution for
the analysis lifecycle:

1. data collection
2. data transformation
3. visualization/publication

all in very accessible and simple-to-use libraries. This is a  **fully reproducible and automated workstream**
which should greatly reduce the need to redo simple tasks over and over.

The library has also been designed so that charts can be exported and imported for example in json format.
This would allow abstracting the features contained in the chart (such as colors, widths, etc.) and writing them to a file,
then later on when the data has been updated, to generate the chart using those features.
For this purpose many of the functions like `lines()` are able to receive the inputs in a `dict` format.
Full use of this feature would allow this library to be integrated into various data formats such as DMXe, etc.

<span style="color:red;">TODO: EXPAND ON</span>

## Gallery {#sec-gallery}

Below are several examples along with the code used to generate them.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Simple line chart

This is a simple line chart that is created with one command.
It uses the default IMF Article IV color scheme, so no line colors need to be specified.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines
#| fig-cap: "Simple line chart."

# Get data from Haver (need imf_datatools)
col1, col2 = 'Unemployment rate', 'Combined rate of underemployment and unemployment (rhs)'
dict_series = {'S186ELUR@EMERGEMA' : col1,
	       'S186EUDR@EMERGEMA' : col2
               }
datafilename = 'data/unemp.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(datafilename):
    df = None
    for series in dict_series:
        _df = imf_datatools.get_haver_data(series)
        _df.columns = [dict_series[series]]
        # Merge
        if df is None:
            df = _df
        else:
            df = df.merge(_df, left_index=True, right_index=True, how='outer')
    df.to_csv(datafilename)
else:
    df = pd.read_csv(datafilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
# Create Chart object
chart = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns[0], rlinecols=df.columns[1],
               title='Labor Utilization', subtitle='(Percent)',
               xrange='2023-01:2025-08',
               yrange=[8, 11],
               )
chart
```

### Chart with line and bars

A chart that has a line and stacked bars.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_bars
#| fig-cap: "A chart with a line and bars."
infilename = 'data/turkey_page4/fig4_chart1_exp.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
cols = df.columns
linecol = 'Primary exp.'
barcols = list(df.columns)
barcols.remove(linecol)

# Create Chart object
attrs = {linecol             : {'linewidth' : 4, 'color' : IMFBLACK},
         'Personnel'         : {'color' : '#004C97', 'edgecolor' : 'red'},
         'Goods & services'  : {'color' : '#009CDE'},
         'Current transfers' : {'color' : '#CAEDFE'},
         'Capital exp.'      : {'color' : '#FF8200'},
         'Other' : {'color'  : '#DA291C'},                         
         }

chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               attrs=attrs,
               topxaxis='left',
               title='Central Government: Primary Expenditure Components',
               subtitle='(Percent of GDP)',
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
chart1
```

### Area chart

An area chart along with a line.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_area
#| fig-cap: "A chart with a line and bars."
infilename = 'data/turkey_page5/fig5_chart6_gross_international_reserves.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
gircol = 'Gross International Reserves (GIR)'
df.rename({gircol + ' (rhs)' :  gircol}, axis=1, inplace=True)

# Create Chart object
attrs = {gircol  : {'color' : '#416FA6'},
         'Gold'  : {'color' : '#CAEDFE'},
         'FX'    : {'color' : '#009CDE'}}

chart6 = Chart(df, linecols=gircol, areacols=['FX', 'Gold'],
               attrs=attrs,
               topxaxis='right',
               title='Gross International Reserves',
               subtitle='(Billions USD)',
               xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[0, 200], ryrange=[-120, 40])
```

### Step chart

When drawing lines, set the option `drawstyle` to `steps-post` to set the line style to be steps.
This is useful for example when plotting the monetary policy rate.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_steps
#| fig-cap: "Line chart shown as steps."

infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart3_mp.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])

attrs = {'Policy rate' : {'color' : IMFBLUE,
                          'drawstyle' : 'steps-post'},
         'Inflation'   : {'color' : IMFRED},
        }

# Create Chart object
chart3 = Chart(df, linecols=['Policy rate', 'Inflation'],
               attrs=attrs,
               title='steps',
               xrange='2023-01:', yrange=[0, 90])
```

### Chart with bars and right lines

A chart with bars on the left y-axis and a line on the right y-axis.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_bars_right
#| fig-cap: "Chart with bars on left y-axis, lines on right y-axis."

# Get data
dict_series = {'N186FPAP@EMERGEMA' : 'Return to asset',
               'N186FNIE@EMERGEMA' : 'Return to equity'}
datafilename = 'data/banks.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(datafilename):
    df = None
    for series in dict_series:
        _df = imf_datatools.get_haver_data(series)
        _df.columns = [dict_series[series]]
        if df is None:
            df = _df
        else:
            df = df.merge(_df, left_index=True, right_index=True, how='outer')
    df.to_csv(datafilename)
else:
    df = pd.read_csv(datafilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
# Take 12-month rolling mean
df = df.rolling(12).mean()

attrs = {'Return to asset' : {'color' : IMFBLUE},
         'Return to equity' : {'color' : IMFRED}}

# Create Chart object
chart = Chart(df, barcols='Return to asset', rlinecols='Return to equity',
              topxaxis='right',
              barlinewidth=0,
              attrs=attrs,
              title='Bank Profitability 1/',
              subtitle='(Percent, 12M moving average)',
              xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[0, 3], ryrange=[0, 30],
              legend_bottom=0.75, legend_left=0.55)

chart
```

### Scatter chart

```{python}
#| label: gallery_scatter
#| fig-cap: "A complicated plot with lines, bars and arrow, text."
infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart4_inflation_forecast.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])

attrs = {'Jul-23' : {'linewidth' : 0,
                     'color' : IMFBLACK,
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : 'o',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : AXISGRAY,
                     'markeredgewidth' : 0,
                     'markeredgecolor' : AXISGRAY},
         'Nov-23' : {'linewidth' : 4,
                     'color' : IMFRED,
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : 'o',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : IMFRED,
                     'markeredgewidth' : 0,
                     'markeredgecolor' : IMFRED},
         'Feb-24' : {'linewidth' : 0,
                     'color' : '#89A54E',
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : '^',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : '#89A54E',
                     'markeredgewidth' : 0,
                     'markeredgecolor' : '#89A54E'},
         'May-24' : {'linewidth' : 0,
                     'color' : '#6E548D',
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : 'x',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : '#6E548D',
                     'markeredgewidth' : 2,
                     'markeredgecolor' : '#6E548D'},
         'Aug-24' : {'linewidth' : 0,
                     'color' : '#3D96AE',
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : 'x|',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : '#3D96AE',
                     'markeredgewidth' : 2,
                     'markeredgecolor' : '#3D96AE'},
         'Nov-24' : {'linewidth' : 4,
                     'color' : '#FFC000',
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : 'o',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : '#FFC000',
                     'markeredgewidth' : 0,
                     'markeredgecolor' : '#FFC000'},
         'Feb-25' : {'linewidth' : 0,
                     'color' : IMFBLACK,
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : '+',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : IMFBLACK,
                     'markeredgewidth' : 2,
                     'markeredgecolor' : IMFBLACK},
         'May-25' : {'linewidth' : 0,
                     'color' : '#FFC000',
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : '_',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : '#C55A11',
                     'markeredgewidth' : 1,
                     'markeredgecolor' : '#C55A11'},
         'Aug-25' : {'linewidth' : 4,
                     'color' : '#7030A0',
                     # 'linestyle' : '--',
                     'marker' : 's',
                     'markersize' : 10,
                     'markerfacecolor' : '#7030A0',
                     'markeredgewidth' : 0,
                     'markeredgecolor' : '#7030A0'},
}

# Create Chart object
chart4 = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns,
               attrs=attrs,
               title="CBRT's Inflation Forecast",
               subtitle='(Percent, end-year inflation)',
               ncol_legend=2, legend_left=0.45, legend_width=0.40,
               yrange=[0, 70])
chart4
```

### Categorical bar chart

```{python}
#| label: gallery_categorical_bars
#| fig-cap: "A complicated plot with lines, bars and arrow, text."
# Read in data
infilename = 'data/turkey_page5/fig5_chart3_terms_of_trade.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0)
attrs = {'shock' : {'color' : '#BFBFBF',
                    'barcolors' : [{'TUR' : 'red'},
                                  ]
                   }
        }
# Create Chart object
chart3 = Chart(df, barcols=df.columns, barstack=False, # baraxis='right',
               attrs=attrs,
               barlinewidth=0, xtickangle=90, xticklength=0,
               title='Terms of Trade Shock, 2025Q2 or Latest',
               subtitle='(Percent change from 2023 average)',
               yrange=[-15, 15],
               show_legend=False)
chart3
```

### Chart with lines, shading, fill

```{python}
#| label: gallery_extras
#| fig-cap: "A chart with lines, shading, and filling."

# Read in data
infilename = 'data/turkey_page1/fig1_chart1_gdp.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
cols = df.columns
gdpcol = cols[0]

# Add "forecast" around gdpcol
df['25'] = df[gdpcol] - 1.0 * np.random.rand(len(df))
df['75'] = df[gdpcol] + 1.0 * np.random.rand(len(df))
df['5']  = df['25'] - 4.  * np.random.rand(len(df))
df['95'] = df['75'] + 4.  * np.random.rand(len(df))

df.loc[:'2022Q1', ['5', '25', '75', '95']] = np.nan

attrs = {gdpcol : {'linewidth' : 2,
                   'color' : '#004C97',
                   'marker' : 'o',
                   'markersize' : 4}
         }
kw_hline = {'y' : 1.4, 'xrange' : '2023Q1:' + df.index[-1].strftime('%Y-%m'),
            'color' : IMFBLACK, 'linewidth' : 1.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'dashes' : [10, 4]}

kw_vline = {'x' : '2020-03',
            'color' : IMFRED, 'linewidth' : 1.5, 'linestyle' : '--'}

kw_fills  = [{'lo' : '25',   'hi' : gdpcol, 'color' : '#CAEDFE'},
             {'lo' : gdpcol, 'hi' : '75',   'color' : '#CAEDFE'},
             {'lo' : '5',   'hi' : '25',    'color' : '#004C97'},
             {'lo' : '75', 'hi' : '95',     'color' : '#004C97'},
            ]

kw_vrect = {'xmin' : '2021Q1', 'xmax' : '2022Q1', 'color' : 'grey', 'alpha' : 0.3}

chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=gdpcol,
               attrs=attrs,
               title='Real GDP Growth and Forecast',
               subtitle='(Percent, q/q)',
               xrange='2020Q1:', yrange=[-12, 18],
               hlines=[kw_hline],
               vlines=[kw_vline],
               vrects=[kw_vrect],
               fills=kw_fills,
               )
chart1
```

### Chart with lines, bars, arrows, text

```{python}
#| label: gallery_gdp
#| fig-cap: "A complicated plot with lines, bars and arrow, text."

# This file has a lot of transformations so copy the data file into the repository.
# To re-generate run examples/get_data_turkey_page1.py and examples/examples_turkey_page1.py.
infilename = 'data/turkey_page1/fig1_chart1_gdp.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist, run example files for Turkey page 1')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
cols = df.columns
linecol = cols[0]
barcols = cols[1:]

# Create Chart object

attrs = {linecol : {'linewidth' : 4,
                    'color' : '#004C97',
                    # 'linestyle' : '--',
                    'marker' : 'o',
                    'markersize' : 10,
                    'markerfacecolor' : 'white',
                    'markeredgewidth' : 4,
                    'markeredgecolor' : '#004C97'},
         'Consumption (private)' : {'color' : '#4B8CAD', 'hatchcolor' : IMFBLUE, 'hatch' : '\\\\'},
         'Consumption (public)' : {'color' : 'white', 'hatch' : '\\\\\\', 'hatchcolor' : IMFBLACK},
         'Net exports' : {'color' : IMFGREY},
         'Inventories/discrepancy' : {'color' : IMFRED},
         'Fixed investment' : {'color' : 'white',
                               'hatch' : '///',
                               'hatchcolor' : IMFGREEN,
                               'hatchwidth' : 10}
         }

chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               attrs=attrs,
               topxaxis='left',
               title='Contributions to Real GDP Growth',
               subtitle='(Percent, q/q)',
               xrange='2023Q1:', yrange=[-5, 9.5], ryrange=[-5, 9.5],
               ncol_legend=2)

# Add hline
chart1.hline(y=1.4, xrange='2023Q1:' + df.index[-1].strftime('%Y-%m'),
             # xrange=[0.05, 0.95], coordinates='axis',
             color=IMFBLACK, linewidth=2.5, linestyle='--', dashes=[10, 4])

# Add text
text = '''Real GDP growth\nlong-run average\n(1.4 q/q)'''
chart1.text(pd.Timestamp('2024Q3'), 6.5, text=text, fontweight='bold', color='#004C97')

# Add arrow
chart1.arrow(head=[pd.Timestamp('2025Q1'), 1.5],
             tail=[pd.Timestamp('2025Q1'), 5],
             color='#004C97')
chart1
```

:::

## About this Document

This document was generated by [Quarto](https://quarto.org/){target="_blank"}, a document generation program.
To generate this document yourself, open Command Prompt and do the following.
It is assumed you have R and RStudio installed from the Software Center, which installs Quarto.

```bash
cd C:\ProgramData\Python3\Lib\site-packages\imfcharts\doc
# If necessary specify full path to quarto
# C:\Program Files\RStudio\resources\app\bin\quarto\bin\quarto.exe render imfcharts.qmd
quarto render imfcharts.qmd
```

and this will generate the HTML and Word output files.

All of the code used to generate the output charts are embedded in the HTML version
of this document, and can be pasted and used directly (as long as the corresponding data is also copied appropriately).
To download the data, the `imf_datatools` library is needed, see [the website](http://datatools) for instructions.

<!------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------>
# Basic Usage {#sec-basics}

Below basic usage of the `imfcharts` package are shown.
For most purposes it should be possible to create a `Chart` object with a single call to the `Chart()` function
by specifying the data to be charted (a `pandas.DataFrame`) and the columns to be shown as lines, bars, area, fills, along with
arguments to specify the title, axis ranges, etc.
It is possible to modify a generated chart by calling additional functions on it such as

```{python}
#| label: basics-first
#| code-fold: show
#| eval: false

# Add bars
chart.bars(data=df, cols='Inflation')

# Set x-axis range
chart.set_xrange([0, 3])

# Set title
chart.set_title('Domestic Growth (Annual)')

# Add horizontal band
chart.hrect(y0=0, y1=3, color='grey', alpha=0.3)
```

In all functions there is a natural and default order of how style features are determined.
The initial settings are determined by the style being used, which will default to `imf-articleiv.mplstyle` and
can also be set manually with

```python
from imfcharts import *
set_style('imf-articleiv')
```

This is a [`Matplotlib` style sheet](https://matplotlib.org/stable/users/explain/customizing.html){target="_blank"}
which specifies chart defaults like title fonts, default colors, tick lengths, etc.
When no options are specified, options specified in this style sheet will be used, so switching to
another style sheet like that for WEO would change to the default WEO color scheme.

The second level, if available is by specifying a characteristic for a specific chart, typically at creation.
For example, to keep all of the Article IV style except the default colors used, create a `Chart` with
```python
chart = Chart(colorcycle=['red', 'green', 'blue'], linewidth=3)
````
and this will generate a chart that cycles through the specified red, green, blue colors for each object plotted,
and set all line charts to have a linewidth of 3 instead of the default `axes.linewidth: 1` specified in the Article IV style file.

The third level is to specify each characteristic at the individual level, typically using the `attrs` argument:
```python
chart = Chart(df, linecols='GDP',, rlinecols='Unemployment',
              linewidth=3,
              attrs={'GDP'          : {'color' : 'black'}},
                     'Unemployment' : {'color' : 'red', 'linewidth' : 1}}
                    }
```
which will override the style settings and any class-level settings if specified.
For example in the above, the style file specifies a linewidth of 1, but this is overridden by
the class-level settings of `linewidth=3`, and this is further overridden by the `attrs` argument
for each line. The line for `'GDP'` will therefore have a linewidth of 2, and `'Unemployment'` will have a linewidth of 1.

While the `Chart()` creation function conveniently provides a way to specify almost all possible input parameters
so that a chart can be created with one call, internally it will call functions like `lines()` and `bars()` which
ensures that the output is consistent between specifying the lines and bars at creation time or adding them later.

It should also be noted that when creating a `Chart` object and adding lines, bars, and area,
the order in which these are drawn is

1. area
2. bars
3. lines

since this is the natural way these would be shown (i.e., area graphs would be in the background,
bars would be shown on top, and lines are shown on top of both so they are not obstructed).
The `colorcycle` argument specifies the colors to cycle through as each component is drawn,
and is shared by lines, bars and area graphs. So for example in the following:
```python
chart = Chart(df, linecols='line', barcols=['bar1', 'bar2'], area=['area1', 'area2'])
```
the first two colors of the color cycle will be used for the area graph columns `'area1'` and `'area2'`,
and the third and fourth colors will be used for the bar columns `'bar1'` and `'bar2'`,
and lastly the fifth color will be used for the line column `'line'`.
Since it is a color cycle, if the number of colors specified is less than the total number of columns
the colors will be recycled. In general the `colorcycle` argument which can be provided when creating
the `Chart` object should have at least as many colors as the number of columns to be shown.
The order of plotting for a variable like `linecols` will be as it is specified, regardless of any order
within the original `pandas.DataFrame` column order.

The below subsections first explain the basics of creating lines, bars and area charts.
In each of these cases they can be specified directly when creating the `Chart` object,
or can be called later with functions like `chart.lines()`. Next, adding annotations such as
horizontal/vertical lines and rectangles are shown in @sec-basics-lines_rects, followed by
adjusting titles, legends, and showing, saving the chart in sec-basics-additional.

## Line Charts {#sec-basics-lines}

The most straightforward use case is to create a line chart, typically a time series chart.
The `Chart()` function will take in a `pandas.DataFrame` and argument `linecols` which can either be a
`str` specifying a column name or an iterable of column names.

```{python}
#| label: lines
#| fig-cap: "A plot with lines"
#| code-fold: show
# Read in data
infilename = 'data/turkey_page1/fig1_chart2_labor.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
unemp = df.columns[0]
combined = df.columns[1]

# Create Chart object
chart2 = Chart(df, linecols=unemp, rlinecols=combined,
               title='Labor Utilization',
               subtitle='(Percent)',
               xrange='2023-01:2025-08',
               yrange=[8, 11],
               )
chart2
```

Note that in the above example, the argument `linecols` and `rlinecols` are used
to specify the columns to be plotted on the left and right y-axes, respectively,
and they are plotted with different scales.
Furthermore, the colors have defaulted to the IMF Article IV style, showing as the usual red and blue colors.

To further customize the lines, the `attrs` option can be used which is a `dict` between each
column to be plotted and its attributes. For example, to change the line colors from the defaults, do

```{python}
#| label: lines_color
#| fig-cap: "Specify line colors."
#| code-fold: show

# Create Chart object
chart2 = Chart(df, linecols=unemp, rlinecols=combined,
               attrs={unemp : {'color' : 'magenta'},
                      combined : {'color' : 'skyblue'}},
               title='Labor Utilization',
               subtitle='(Percent)',
               xrange='2023-01:2025-08',
               yrange=[8, 11],
               )
chart2
```

In the above the variable `attrs` is a `dict` with keys corresponding to the line columns.
The colors of each line are specified with the key `color` and corresponding color.
For a list of valid numbers see @sec-basics-misc-colors.

The available keys for a column within `attrs` are:

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| color | Line color, specify as valid color given in @sec-basics-misc-colors |
| linewidth | Line width of marker |
| dashes | Line style, default is `'-'` for solid lines, specify a [valid line style](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/linestyles.html){target="_blank"} |
| linestyle | A valid `Matplotlib` line style specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/linestyles.html){target="_blank"}. Special implementations such as "imfdash", "imfround" are implemented which mimic Excel styles. |
| marker | Specify the marker (default no marker) using one of symbols [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/api/markers_api.html){target="_blank"}. Specifying multiple symbols together like "x\|" will combine the symbols in the legend. |
| markersize | Size of marker |
| markerfacecolor | Face color of marker |
| markeredgecolor | Edge color of marker |
| markeredgewidth | Edge width of marker |
| drawstyles | Can be used to draw step-like lines which are used for example for policy rates, options are 'default', 'steps', 'steps-pre', 'steps-mid', 'steps-post'; typically for a policy rate chart the option 'steps-pre' would be used. |
| dashes, dash_capstyle | Advanced options to specify cap styles |
| legend | If `False`, do not show this entry in the legend |

A fuller example is below.
```{python}
#| label: lines_all_options
#| fig-cap: "Full example of line options."
#| code-fold: show

attrs = {unemp : {'color' : (0.22, 0.5, 0.9, 0.7),
                  'linestyle' : 'dashed',
                  'linewidth' : 3,
                  'marker' : 'x|', # combine markers
                  'markeredgecolor' : 'purple',
                  'markersize' : 12,
                  'markeredgewidth' : 2,
                 },
         combined : {'color' : 'salmon',
                     'linestyle' : 'dotted',
                     'legend' : False, # don't show in legend
                     'marker' : 'o',
                     'markersize' : 12,
                     'markerfacecolor' : '#00CCCC',
                     'markeredgecolor' : '#AB2323',
                     'markeredgewidth' : 3,
	            }
	}

# Create Chart object
chart2 = Chart(df, linecols=unemp, rlinecols=combined,
               attrs=attrs,
               title='Labor Utilization',
               subtitle='(Percent)',
               xrange='2023-01:2025-08',
               yrange=[8, 11],
               )
chart2
```

Internally, lines are added with the function `Chart.lines()`, options for this function are

| Argument | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| data | None | Data to use, in the form of a `pandas.DataFrame`. If no data is specified, will try to use any data that was previously specified to create this chart |
| cols | None | The columns in the data to be charted as lines. Note that when calling `Chart()`, this option is `barcols`. An iterable of column names, or if a single column can use a `str`.|
| indexcol | None | By default the input `pandas.DataFrame`'s index is used as the x-axis. If this is a column in the DataFrame, specify using this option. |
| axis | 'left' | Specify which y-axis to plot on, choose either 'left' or 'right' |
| colorcycle | None | Specify an iterable of valid colors to be used as the color cycle, if not specified will default to what is specified in the current style |
| linewidth | None | If specified, override what is specified in the current style and apply as default to all lines drawn in this call |
| linebreaks | False | If `linebreaks=False`, then connect the lines even if there are NA values. |
| drawstyle | None | Set all lines to use the specified drawstyle for example to create step charts, see example [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/step_demo.html){target="_blank"} |
| attrs | None | Attributes for individual columns. |
| xrange | None | Specify x-axis range. See @sec-basics-format_axes. |
| margins | None | If specified, set how additional margins are added. Default is 'auto' which will automatically adjust. Can also specify number of days as integers. |
| xformat | None | Specify how x-axis is formatted for a time series plot. Default is to check the frequency of the data and automatically determine. Otherwise specify a frequency like 'D', 'W', 'M', 'Q', 'A' for daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly or annual frequency. |
| yrange | None | Override existing `yrange` of this object and set y-axis range |
| ryrange | None | Override existing `ryrange` of this object and set right y-axis range |
| debug | False | Show information when running. |

When drawing lines, set the option `drawstyle` to `steps-post` to set the line style to be steps.
This is useful for example when plotting the monetary policy rate.

Below is a gallery of various line charts and options.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### No options (Article IV style)

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_nooptions
#| fig-cap: "Line chart (no options)."
#| code-fold: show

infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart3_mp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])

chart = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns,
              title='No Options')
```

### Class-level options

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_classoptions
#| fig-cap: "Line chart with class-level options."
#| code-fold: show

infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart3_mp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])

chart = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns,
              title='Class-level Options',
              colorcycle=['red', 'green', 'blue'],
              linewidth=3,
              xrange='2023-01:', yrange=[0, 90])
```

### Attributes

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_attrs
#| fig-cap: "Line chart with individual attributes specified."
#| code-fold: show

infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart3_mp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
attrs = {'Policy rate' : {'color' : IMFBLUE, 'linewidth' : 2},
         'Inflation' : {'color' : IMFRED, 'marker' : 'o', 'markersize' : 3, 'markerfacecolor' : 'red', 'markeredgecolor' : 'black'},
         'Inflation expectation' : {'color' : IMFBLACK, 'linestyle' : '--'}}

chart = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns,
              title='Attributes',
              attrs=attrs,
              xrange='2023-01:', yrange=[0, 90])
```

### Add to existing `Chart` with `lines()`

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_calllines
#| fig-cap: "Add lines to existing chart."
#| code-fold: show

infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart3_mp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
chart = Chart(title='Class-level Options')

# Call lines on existing chart
attrs = {'Policy rate' : {'color' : IMFBLUE, 'linewidth' : 2},
         'Inflation' : {'color' : IMFRED, 'marker' : 'o', 'markersize' : 3, 'markerfacecolor' : 'red', 'markeredgecolor' : 'black'},
         'Inflation expectation' : {'color' : IMFBLACK, 'linestyle' : '--'}}
chart.lines(data=df, cols=df.columns,
            attrs=attrs,
            xrange='2023-01:', yrange=[0, 90])
```

### Step chart

When drawing lines, set the option `drawstyle` to `steps-post` to set the line style to be steps.
This is useful for example when plotting the monetary policy rate.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_steps
#| fig-cap: "Line chart shown as steps."
#| code-fold: show

infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart3_mp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])

attrs = {'Policy rate' : {'color' : IMFBLUE,
                          'drawstyle' : 'steps-post'},
         'Inflation'   : {'color' : IMFRED},
        }

# Create Chart object
chart = Chart(df, linecols=['Policy rate', 'Inflation'],
              attrs=attrs,
              title='steps',
              xrange='2023-01:', yrange=[0, 90])
```

### Use `dict` as Input to `lines()`

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_kwargs
#| fig-cap: "Add lines to existing chart using `dict`."
#| code-fold: show

infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart3_mp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
chart = Chart(title='Class-level Options')

# Call lines on existing chart
attrs = {'Policy rate' : {'color' : IMFBLUE, 'linewidth' : 2},
         'Inflation' : {'color' : IMFRED, 'marker' : 'o', 'markersize' : 3, 'markerfacecolor' : 'red', 'markeredgecolor' : 'black'},
         'Inflation expectation' : {'color' : IMFBLACK, 'linestyle' : '--'}}
kwargs = {'data' : df, 'cols' : df.columns,
          'attrs' : attrs,
          'xrange' : '2023-01:', 'yrange' : [0, 90]}
chart.lines(**kwargs)
```

:::


## Add Bars {#sec-basics-bars}

To add bars, use the argument `barcols` in `Chart()` or use the function `Chart.bars()` on an existing `Chart` with the argument `cols`.
The argument can either be a `str` specifying a single column or an iterable of multiple column names,
and the default is to have all bars stacked.

An example is shown below.

```{python}
#| label: basics_barchart
#| fig-cap: "Generate bar chart data."
datafilename = 'data/gdp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(datafilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
df['Consumption (private)'] = df['Consumption1'] + df['Consumption2']
df['Net exports'] = df['Exports'] - df['Imports']
df['Inventories/discrepancy'] = df['Real GDP growth'] - df['Consumption (private)'] - df['Consumption (public)'] - df['Fixed investment'] - df['Net exports']

gdpcol = 'Real GDP growth'
barcols = ['Consumption (private)', 'Consumption (public)', 'Net exports', 'Inventories/discrepancy', 'Fixed investment']
df = df[[gdpcol] + barcols].copy()
# Normalize change by previous GDP value
for barcol in barcols:
    df[barcol] = 100. * df[barcol].diff() / df[gdpcol].shift()
# Calculate %pop for GDP
df[gdpcol] = imf_datatools.dataframe_utilities.calc_pop(df[gdpcol])
```

```{python}
#| label: barchart
#| fig-cap: "Example bar chart."
#| code-fold: show
linecol = cols[0]
barcols = cols[1:]

# Create Chart object
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               topxaxis='left',
               title='Contributions to Real GDP Growth',
               subtitle='(Percent, q/q)',
               xrange='2023Q1:', yrange=[-5, 9.5], ryrange=[-5, 9.5],
               ncol_legend=2)
chart1
```

Similar to plotting lines, an option `attrs` can be passed in to specify the attributes of each bar.
The default is to follow the color scheme that is specified in the current style, and we see the bars
having the familiar Article IV colors.


The available options in `attrs` for bars is as follows:

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| color | The color of bars for this column. |
| width | Specify width of bars. If time series, in units of days. |
| offset | Additional offset for bars. If time series, in units of days. |
| edgecolor | Edge colors of bars. |
| hatchcolor | Specify colors for any hatches |
| hatch | Specify a valid hatch specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/shapes_and_collections/hatch_style_reference.html){target="_blank"} |
| hatchwidth | Specify hatch bar width |
| edgecolor | Specify color of lines for the edges of the bars. |
| legend | If `False`, do not add to legend. |
| barcolors | Specify color of individual colors of bars using the index of the data. See example below. |
| offset | If `stack=False`, specify custom offset of x-axis position for this column. |

The `attrs` keywords are set so that when possible, for example for `color` it is the same as with those for when calling `lines()`,
so that it is easy to switch from a line chart to a bar chart using the same `attrs` if the attributes are the same.

Also note that the default is to have the edges of bars in black. This color can be modfiied with `baredgecolor` for each column,
and to remove use the option `barlinewidth=0` when calling `Chart()` or `Chart.bars()`.

An example with customized bar `attrs` is below.

```{python}
#| label: barchart_options
#| fig-cap: "Example bar chart."

# Create Chart object
attrs = {linecol : {'linewidth' : 4,
                    'color' : '#004C97',
                    # 'linestyle' : '--',
                    'marker' : 'o',
                    'markersize' : 10,
                    'markerfacecolor' : 'white',
                    'markeredgewidth' : 4,
                    'markeredgecolor' : '#004C97'},
         'Consumption (private)'   : {'color' : '#4B8CAD', 'barhatchcolor' : IMFBLUE, 'barhatch' : '\\\\'},
         'Consumption (public)'    : {'color' : 'white', 'barhatch' : '\\\\\\', 'barhatchcolor' : IMFBLACK},
         'Net exports'             : {'color' : IMFGREY},
         'Inventories/discrepancy' : {'color' : IMFRED},
         'Fixed investment'        : {'color' : 'white', 'barhatch' : '///','barhatchcolor' : IMFGREEN, 'barhatchwidth' : 10}
         }

chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               attrs=attrs,
               topxaxis='left',
               title='Contributions to Real GDP Growth',
               subtitle='(Percent, q/q)',
               xrange='2023Q1:', yrange=[-5, 9.5], ryrange=[-5, 9.5],
               ncol_legend=2)
chart1
```

Internally, bar charts are created with the function `chart.bars()`.
As with `lines()`, the attributes of each bar are determined in the order of

1. Style options
2. Class-level options specified either when calling `Chart()` or `Chart.bars()`
3. Individual column level using `attrs`.

Options for `Chart.bars()` are:

| Argument | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| data | None | Data to use, in the form of a `pandas.DataFrame`. If no data is specified, will try to use any data that was previously specified to create this chart |
| cols | None | The columns in the data to be charted as lines. Note that when calling `Chart()`, this option is `linecols` and `rlinecols` to distinguish from columns for bars and area graphs. An iterable of column names, or if a single column can use a `str`.|
| indexcol | None | By default the input `pandas.DataFrame`'s index is used as the x-axis. If this is a column in the DataFrame, specify using this option. |
| axis | None | Specify which y-axis to plot on. Note that when calling `Chart()` this is specified as `baraxis` |
| stack | True | Specify whether to stack bars. If `True`, all bars are stacked on top of each other (default), otherwise they are staggered. when calling `Chart()` this is specified as `barstack`. |
| total_barwidth |None | When not plotting bars as a time series, specify the width of each bar, default is 0.9. |
| linewidth |None | Width of edges of bars. When calling `Chart()` this is specified as `barlinewidth`. |
| edgecolor | None | Edge color for bars. If not specified defaults to style default (black for Article IV). When calling `Chart()` this is specified as `baredgecolor`. |
| attrs | None | Attributes for individual columns. |
| xrange | None | Specify x-axis range. See @sec-basics-format_axes. |
| margins | None | If specified, set how additional margins are added. Default is 'auto' which will automatically adjust. Can also specify number of days as integers. |
| xformat | None | Specify how x-axis is formatted for a time series plot. Default is to check the frequency of the data and automatically determine. Otherwise specify a frequency like 'D', 'W', 'M', 'Q', 'A' for daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly or annual frequency. |
| yrange | None | Override existing `yrange` of this object and set y-axis range |
| ryrange | None | Override existing `ryrange` of this object and set right y-axis range |
| debug | False | Show information when running. |

Again, note that many options are the same as for `Chart.lines()`.
Below is a gallery of bar charts with various options.

::: {.panel-tabset}

### No options (Article IV style)

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_nooptions
#| fig-cap: "Bar chart (no options)."
#| code-fold: show
infilename = 'data/turkey_page4/fig4_chart1_exp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
cols = df.columns
linecol = 'Primary exp.'
barcols = list(df.columns)
barcols.remove(linecol)
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               title='no options',
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Color Cycle

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_colorcycle
#| fig-cap: "Color cycle."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               title='color cycle',
               colorcycle=['red', 'green', 'blue', 'black'],
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Axis Right

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_axis_right
#| fig-cap: "Axis right."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               title='axis right',
               baraxis='right',
               xrange='2018-01:', ryrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Bar Edge Color/Width

In the below `baredgecolor` is set to `'gold'` and the edge line width is given with `barlinewidth=3`.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_edges
#| fig-cap: ":Edge color and width."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               title='bar edges',
               baredgecolor='gold',
               barlinewidth=3,
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Margins

In the below `margins` is set to 170 days.
Since for annual frequency data the bar is set by default to 335 days,
this removes most of the side margin for the chart.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_margins
#| fig-cap: "Specify margins."
#| code-fold: show
# Create Chart object
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               title='margins',
               margins=120,
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Attributes

In the below a `dict` called `attrs` is passed in to specify the colors of each bar.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_attrs
#| fig-cap: "Specify attributes."
#| code-fold: show
attrs = {linecol : {'linewidth' : 4, 'color' : IMFBLACK},
         'Personnel' : {'color' : '#004C97'},
         'Goods & services' : {'color' : '#009CDE'},
         'Current transfers' : {'color' : '#CAEDFE'},
         'Capital exp.' : {'color' : '#FF8200'},
         'Other' : {'color' : '#DA291C'},                         
         }

# Create Chart object
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               title='attrs',
               attrs=attrs,
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Add Bars From a `dict`

In the below the function `bars()` is called with a `dict` which is passed in as `**kwargs**`.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_kwargs
#| fig-cap: "Create from `dict`."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(title='kwargs',
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)

# Add line using dict
kwargs_lines = {'data' : df,
                'cols' : linecol,
                'attrs' : attrs}
chart1.lines(**kwargs_lines)

# Add bars using dict
kwargs = {'data' : df,
          'cols' : barcols,
          'attrs' : attrs,
          }
chart1.bars(**kwargs)
```

### Unstacked

In the below the option `stack=False` is passed in.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_unstacked
#| fig-cap: "Unstacked."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               barstack=False,
               attrs=attrs,
               title='stack=False',
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Specify individual colors

To specify colors for individaul bars, set them in `attrs` by
specifying for that column the key `"barcolor"` and passing in a `list` of `dicts` between
the index and the color.

In the below example, the column "Personnel" will get a red color for the periods 2022 and 2024.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_individual_colors
#| fig-cap: "Individual colors."
#| code-fold: show

attrs = {linecol : {'linewidth' : 4, 'color' : IMFBLACK},
         'Personnel' : {'color' : '#004C97', 'baredgecolor' : 'red',
                        'barcolors' : [{'2022' : 'red'}, {'2024' : 'red'}]},
         'Goods & services' : {'color' : '#009CDE'},
         'Current transfers' : {'color' : '#CAEDFE'},
         'Capital exp.' : {'color' : '#FF8200'},
         'Other' : {'color' : '#DA291C'},                         
         }

chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, barcols=barcols,
               attrs=attrs,
               title='individual colors',
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Categorical, unstacked

In the below a `pandas.DataFrame` with a categorical index is shown as a bar chart that has `stack=False`.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_categorical
#| fig-cap: "Categorical."
#| code-fold: show
infilename = 'data/turkey_page5/fig5_chart3_terms_of_trade.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0)

chart3 = Chart(df, barcols=df.columns, barstack=False,
               xtickangle=90, xticklength=0,
               title='categorical, no stack',
               yrange=[-15, 15])
```

### Categorical, unstacked individual colors

In the below, the bar color for "TUR" is set to red.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_bars_categorical_colors
#| fig-cap: "Categorical with individual colors"
#| code-fold: show
attrs = {'shock' : {'color' : '#BFBFBF',
                    'barcolors' : [{'TUR' : 'red'}]}
        }
chart3 = Chart(df, barcols=df.columns, barstack=False,
               attrs=attrs,
               xtickangle=90, xticklength=0,
               title='categorical, no stack with colors',
               yrange=[-15, 15],
               show_legend=False)
```

:::

## Add Area {#sec-basics-area}

An area graph is very similar to a bar graph, and can be drawn with either the `areacols` option for `Chart()`
or with `Chart.area()` with the `cols` option. As with `lines()` and `bars()`, the option can
be a `str` for a single column or an iterable of multiple column names.

An example is shown below.

```{python}
#| label: basics_areachart
#| fig-cap: "Generate bar chart data."
infilename = 'data/turkey_page4/fig4_chart1_exp.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
cols = df.columns
linecol = 'Primary exp.'
areacols = list(df.columns)
areacols.remove(linecol)
```

```{python}
#| label: areachart
#| fig-cap: "Example area chart."
#| code-fold: show
# Create Chart object
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=linecol, areacols=areacols,
               title='Central Government: Primary Expenditure Components',
               subtitle='(Percent of GDP)',
               xrange='2018-01:', yrange=[0, 30],
               ncol_legend=2)
chart1
```

The chart is identical to that of the chart for no options in the Bar chart gallery (@sec-basics-bars),
except that the stacked bars have been replaced with stacked areas.
Most of the options for area charts are the same as for bar charts, but to allow them to be specified together
when calling from `Chart()`, some input parameters are slightly different, for example the columns are
`areacols` when calling `Chart()` but `cols` when calling `Chart.area()`:
```python
Chart(df, areacols=cols)
```
and
```python
chart = Chart()
chart.area(data=df, cols=cols)
```

One further difference is that since area charts are expected to fill the entire x-axis range,
the `margins` are automatically set to 0 when an area graph is drawn.

Similar to `lines()` and `bars()`, the keyword `attrs` provides a way to control the attributes for each column.
Below are the options for an area graph, many are similar to that for bar graphs.

| Variable | Description |
|---|---|
| color | The color of bars for this column. |
| edgecolor | Edge colors of bars. |
| hatchcolor | Specify colors for any hatches |
| hatch | Specify a valid hatch specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/shapes_and_collections/hatch_style_reference.html){target="_blank"} |
| hatchwidth | Specify hatch bar width |
| alpha | Apply transparency to color of area, specify as float between 0 and 1. |
| edgecolor | Specify color of lines for the edges of the bars. |
| legend | If `False`, do not add to legend. |

All of these options are the same as for `bars()` except for `alpha`.

An example with customized area attrs is below.

```{python}
#| label: area_custom
#| fig-cap: "Customized area chart."

infilename = 'data/turkey_page5/fig5_chart6_gross_international_reserves.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
df.dropna(axis=0, how='all', inplace=True)

# Create Chart object
gircol = 'Gross International Reserves (GIR) (rhs)'
attrs = {gircol : {'color' : '#416FA6'},
         'Gold'  : {'color' : '#CAEDFE',
                   'hatch' : 'blue',
                   'hatch' : '\\\\',
                   'hatchwidth' : 2,
                   'edgecolor' : 'brown',
                   'alpha' : 0.3},
         'FX'    : {'color' : '#009CDE',
                    'hatchcolor' : 'red',
                    'hatch' : '////',
                    'hatchwidth' : 3,
                    'edgecolor' : 'purple'},
        }

chart = Chart(df, linecols=gircol,
              areacols=['FX', 'Gold'],
              attrs=attrs,
              title='Area chart',
              xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[-0.5, 200])
```

Internally, area graphs are created with the function `chart.area()`. As with `lines()` and `bars()`, the attributes of each area
are determined in the order of

1. Style options
2. Class-level options specified either when calling `Chart()` or `Chart.bars()`
3. Individual column level using `attrs`.

| Argument | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| data | None | Data to use, in the form of a `pandas.DataFrame`. If no data is specified, will try to use any data that was previously specified to create this chart |
| cols | None | The columns in the data to be charted as lines. Note that when calling `Chart()`, this option is `areacols` to distinguish from columns for lines and bars. An iterable of column names, or if a single column can use a `str`.|
| indexcol | None | By default the input `pandas.DataFrame`'s index is used as the x-axis. If this is a column in the DataFrame, specify using this option. |
| axis | None | Specify which y-axis to plot on. Note that when calling `Chart()` this is specified as `areaaxis` |
| stack | True | Specify whether to stack bars. If `True`, all bars are stacked on top of each other (default), otherwise they are staggered. when calling `Chart()` this is specified as `areastack`. |
| linewidth |None | Width of edges of bars. When calling `Chart()` this is specified as `arealinewidth`. |
| edgecolor | None | Edge color for bars. If not specified defaults to style default (black for Article IV). When calling `Chart()` this is specified as `areaedgecolor`. |
| attrs | None | Attributes for individual columns. |
| xrange | None | Specify x-axis range. See @sec-basics-format_axes. |
| margins | None | If specified, set how additional margins are added. Default is 'auto' which will automatically adjust. Can also specify number of days as integers. |
| xformat | None | Specify how x-axis is formatted for a time series plot. Default is to check the frequency of the data and automatically determine. Otherwise specify a frequency like 'D', 'W', 'M', 'Q', 'A' for daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly or annual frequency. |
| yrange | None | Override existing `yrange` of this object and set y-axis range |
| ryrange | None | Override existing `ryrange` of this object and set right y-axis range |
| debug | False | Show information when running. |

Again, note that many options are the same as for `Chart.lines()` and especially `Chart.bars()`, so that switching
between bars and area graphs based on aesthetics is easy to do.
Below is a gallery of area charts with various options.

```{python}
#| label: area_data
infilename = 'data/turkey_page5/fig5_chart6_gross_international_reserves.csv'
if not os.path.isfile(infilename):
    print('File ' + infilename + ' does not exist')
    sys.exit()
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
df.dropna(axis=0, how='all', inplace=True)

# Create Chart object
gircol = 'Gross International Reserves (GIR) (rhs)'
areacols = ['FX', 'Gold']
```

::: {.panel-tabset}

### No options (Article IV style)

No options are passed in.
For area charts the margins are by default set to 0 so there is no space outside the plotted range.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_area_nooptions
#| fig-cap: "No options"
#| code-fold: show
# Create Chart object
chart = Chart(df, areacols=areacols,
              title='no options')
chart	      
```

### Color cycle

A color cycle is provided of red, green, blue.
Note that the first two colors are used for the area graphs,
and the third color blue is then used for the line.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_area_colorcycle
#| fig-cap: "Color cycle"
#| code-fold: show
# Create Chart object
chart = Chart(df, linecols=gircol, areacols=areacols,
              colorcycle=['red', 'green', 'blue'],
              title='color cycle')
chart
```

### Axis Right

The area chart is drawn on the right.
Since no y-axis range is specified for either the left or right y-axis,
the ranges do not agree exactly.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_area_axis_right
#| fig-cap: "Axis right."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=gircol, areacols=areacols,
               title='axis right',
               areaaxis='right',
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Area Edge Color/Width

In the below `areaedgecolor` is set to `'gold'` and the edge line width is given with `arealinewidth=3`.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_area_edges
#| fig-cap: "Edge color and width."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=gircol, areacols=areacols,
               title='area edges',
               areaedgecolor='gold',
               arealinewidth=3,
               xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[0, 200],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Attributes

In the below a `dict` called `attrs` is passed in to specify the colors of each area.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_area_attrs
#| fig-cap: "Specify attributes."
#| code-fold: show
attrs = {gircol : {'color' : '#416FA6'},
         'FX' : {'color' : 'yellow',
                 'hatchcolor' : 'red',
                 'hatch' : '////',
                 'hatchwidth' : 3,
                 'edgecolor' : 'purple'},
         'Gold' : {'color' : 'grey',
                   'hatch' : 'blue',
                   'hatch' : '\\\\',
                   'hatchwidth' : 2,
                   'edgecolor' : 'brown',
                   'alpha' : 0.3}
         }

# Create Chart object
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=gircol, areacols=areacols,
               title='attrs',
               attrs=attrs,
               xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[0, 200],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

### Add Area From a `dict`

In the below the function `area()` is called with a `dict` which is passed in as `**kwargs**`.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_area_kwargs
#| fig-cap: "Create from `dict`."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(title='kwargs',
               xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[0, 200],
               ncol_legend=2)

attrs = {linecol : {'linewidth' : 4, 'color' : IMFBLACK},
         'Personnel' : {'color' : '#004C97'},
         'Goods & services' : {'color' : '#009CDE'},
         'Current transfers' : {'color' : '#CAEDFE'},
         'Capital exp.' : {'color' : '#FF8200'},
         'Other' : {'color' : '#DA291C'},                         
         }

# Add area using dict
kwargs = {'data' : df,
          'cols' : areacols,
          'attrs' : attrs,
          }
chart1.area(**kwargs)
```

### Unstacked

In the below the option `stack=False` is passed in.
To make the area graphs easier to see, `alpha=0.3` is also set for all areas.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_area_unstacked
#| fig-cap: "Unstacked."
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=gircol, areacols=areacols,
              colorcycle=['red', 'yellow'],
	       alpha=0.3,
               areastack=False,
               title='stack=False',
               xrange='2022-01:', yrange=[0, 200],
               ncol_legend=2)
```

:::

## Add Horizontal/Vertical Lines and Rectangles {#sec-basics-lines_rects}

In many cases it is convenient to be able to add various auxiliary objects to a chart, such as horizontal, vertical lines and shaded areas (rectangles).
The `imfcharts` provides simple functions to provide these.

```python
chart = Chart(df, linecols=linecols)

# Draw a horizontal line at 3
chart.hline(3)

# Draw a vertical line at 2020-03
chart.vline('2020-03')

# Draw a shaded target range between y = 0 and 1
chart.hrect(0, 1)

# Draw a shaded region between 2020-03 and 2021-06
chart.vrect('2020-03', '2021-06')
```

For horizontal lines and rectangles, the default is to span the entire width of the chart, these are useful for showing a target value or a target band.
For vertical lines and rectangles, the default is to span the entire height of the chart, these are useful for showing a specific date or category, or
a specific period (such are recessions). Under the hood, `hline` and `vline` use `Matplotlib`'s `ax.hline` and `ax.vline`, and `hrect` and `vrect` use
`ax.axhspan` and `ax.axvspan`.

Below are the options for each of these, and a gallery is provided with use cases.

### Horizontal Lines

Horizontal lines can be added to an existing `Chart` object with
```python
chart.hline(y)
```
which will draw a horizontal line at `y` across the entire chart.
Further options listed below allows customization.

| Argument | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
|y       |      | Specify y position where line is drawn in coordinates of data being plotted. |
| xrange | None | Specify x-axis range for horizontal line. If `coordinates` is `'data'` for time series data, this converts the passed in `xrange` into coordinates of the chart. Otherwise the values are fractions of the chart from 0 to 1. |
| coordinates | 'data' | If this is `data` and the chart is plotting a time series, `xrange` will be interpreted as time ranges. |
| color | 'red' | Specify color of line, see sec-basics-misc-colors for valid colors. |
| linewidth |None | Width of edges of line. |
| linestyle | A valid `Matplotlib` line style specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/linestyles.html){target="_blank"}. Special implementations such as "imfdash", "imfround" are implemented which mimic Excel styles. |
| alpha | 1 | Apply transparency to color of area, specify as float between 0 and 1. |
| dashes, dash_capstyle | Advanced options to specify cap styles |
| label | '' | When `legend` is `True`, a legend entry is created using this label.  |
| legend | `False` | If `False`, do not show this entry in the legend |
| zorder | 0 | Specify the drawing order, higher values are shown on top of other objects. |
| debug | False | Show information when running. |

### Horizontal Rectangles

Horizontal rectangles (shades) have similar options as `hline` objects, a difference being that instead of one `y` value
one must specify two values of `ymin` and `ymax` of where the shade is drawn.

In the simplest case
```python
chart.hrect(0, 1)
```
will draw a shaded region between 0 and 1 in the data coordinates.
Additional options are below.

| Argument | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
|ymin | 0 | Specify lower y position where shade is drawn in coordinates of data being plotted. |
|ymax | 0 | Specify higher y position where shade is drawn in coordinates of data being plotted. |
| xrange | None | Specify x-axis range for horizontal line. If `coordinates` is `'data'` for time series data, this converts the passed in `xrange` into coordinates of the chart. Otherwise the values are fractions of the chart from 0 to 1. |
| coordinates | 'data' | If this is `data` and the chart is plotting a time series, `xrange` will be interpreted as time ranges. |
| color | 'red' | Specify color of line, see sec-basics-misc-colors for valid colors. |
| linecolor | 'none' | Specify color of outline of shade. |
| linewidth | 0 | Width of edges of shade. |
| linestyle | A valid `Matplotlib` line style specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/linestyles.html){target="_blank"}. Special implementations such as "imfdash", "imfround" are implemented which mimic Excel styles. |
| alpha | 0.3 | Apply transparency to color of area, specify as float between 0 and 1. |
| dashes, dash_capstyle | Advanced options to specify cap styles |
| hatch | None | Specify hatches to draw |
| label | '' | When `legend` is `True`, a legend entry is created using this label.  |
| legend | `False` | If `False`, do not show this entry in the legend |
| zorder | 0 | Specify the drawing order, higher values are shown on top of other objects. |
| debug | False | Show information when running. |

### Vertical Lines

Horizontal lines can be added to an existing `Chart` object with
```python
chart.vline(x)
```
which will draw a vertical line at `x` for the entire chart.
Further options listed below allows customization.

| Argument | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
|y       |      | Specify y position where line is drawn in coordinates of data being plotted. |
| yrange | None | Specify y-axis range for horizontal line. If `coordinates` is `'data'`, this interprets the values as coordinates of the chart, otherwise the values are fractions of the chart from 0 to 1. |
| coordinates | 'data' | If this is `data`, `yrange` will be interpreted as coordinates of the chart, otherwise as fractions of the chart. |
| color | 'red' | Specify fill color of shade, see sec-basics-misc-colors for valid colors. |
| linewidth | 1 | Width of edges of line. |
| linestyle | '-' | A valid `Matplotlib` line style specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/linestyles.html){target="_blank"}. Special implementations such as "imfdash", "imfround" are implemented which mimic Excel styles. |
| alpha | 1 | Apply transparency to color of area, specify as float between 0 and 1. |
| dashes, dash_capstyle | Advanced options to specify cap styles |
| label | '' | When `legend` is `True`, a legend entry is created using this label.  |
| legend | `False` | If `False`, do not show this entry in the legend |
| zorder | 0 | Specify the drawing order, higher values are shown on top of other objects. |
| debug | False | Show information when running. |

### Vertical Shades

Vertical rectangles (shades) have similar options as `hrect` objects, a difference being that instead of one `y` value
one must specify two values of `ymin` and `ymax` of where the shade is drawn.

In the simplest case
```python
chart.vrect(0, 1)
```
will draw a shaded region between 0 and 1 in the data coordinates.
Additional options are below.

| Argument | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
|ymin | 0 | Specify lower y position where shade is drawn in coordinates of data being plotted. |
|ymax | 0 | Specify higher y position where shade is drawn in coordinates of data being plotted. |
| yrange | None | Specify y-axis range for horizontal line. If `coordinates` is `'data'`, this interprets the values as coordinates of the chart, otherwise the values are fractions of the chart from 0 to 1. |
| coordinates | 'data' | If this is `data`, `yrange` will be interpreted as coordinates of the chart, otherwise as fractions of the chart. |
| color | 'red' | Specify color of line, see sec-basics-misc-colors for valid colors. |
| linecolor | 'none' | Specify color of outline of shade. |
| linewidth |0 | Width of edges of shade. |
| linestyle | A valid `Matplotlib` line style specified [here](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/lines_bars_and_markers/linestyles.html){target="_blank"}. Special implementations such as "imfdash", "imfround" are implemented which mimic Excel styles. |
| alpha | 0.3 | Apply transparency to color of area, specify as float between 0 and 1. |
| dashes, dash_capstyle | Advanced options to specify cap styles |
| hatch | None | Specify hatches to draw |
| label | '' | When `legend` is `True`, a legend entry is created using this label.  |
| legend | `False` | If `False`, do not show this entry in the legend |
| zorder | 0 | Specify the drawing order, higher values are shown on top of other objects. |
| debug | False | Show information when running. |

Below is a gallery showing how to add lines and shades.

::: {.panel-tabset}

```{python}

# Read in data
infilename = 'data/turkey_page2/fig2_chart1_inflation.csv'
df = pd.read_csv(infilename, index_col=0, parse_dates=[0])
```

### No options

No options are passed in for the `hline`, `vline`, `hrect` or `vrect`.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_rects_nooptions
#| fig-cap: "No options"
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns, attrs=attrs,
               title='no options',
               xrange='2017-01:', yrange=[0, 120])

# Add hline
chart1.hline(y=20)

# Add vline
chart1.vline(x='2018-03')

# Add hrect
chart1.hrect(ymin=40, ymax=60)

chart1.vrect(xmin='2020-03', xmax='2022-06')
```

### Plot Options

Various plot options are passed into the  `hline`, `vline`, `hrect` and `vrect` objects.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_rects_options
#| fig-cap: "With options"
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns, attrs=attrs,
               title='with options',
               xrange='2017-01:', yrange=[0, 120])

# Add hline
chart1.hline(y=20, xrange='2020-03:2025-04',
             color='blue', linewidth=1.5, linestyle='--', alpha=0.8)

# Add vline
chart1.vline(x='2018-03', yrange=[30, 100],
             color='green', linewidth=1.5, linestyle='--', alpha=0.8)

# Add hrect
chart1.hrect(ymin=40, ymax=60, xrange='2020-03:2025-04',
             hatch='\\\\', color='yellow', linewidth=1.5, linecolor='orange', linestyle='--', alpha=0.3)

chart1.vrect(xmin='2020-03', xmax='2022-06', yrange=[30, 60],
             hatch='//', color='blue', linewidth=1.5, linecolor='purple', linestyle='dotted', alpha=0.3)
```

### zorder, Legend Entry

The `zorder` of each `hline`, `vline`, `hrect` and `vrect` object are set to 5 so that they are above the data lines,
and a legend entry is added for each object.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_rects_zorder
#| fig-cap: "zorder, legend"
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns, attrs=attrs,
               title='zorder, legend',
               xrange='2017-01:', yrange=[0, 120])

# Add hline
chart1.hline(y=20, xrange='2020-03:2025-04',
             color='blue', linewidth=1.5, linestyle='--', alpha=0.8,
             zorder=5, label='target', legend=True)

# Add vline
chart1.vline(x='2018-03',
             color='blue', linewidth=1.5, linestyle='--', alpha=0.8,
             zorder=5, label='reference period', legend=True)

# Add hrect
chart1.hrect(ymin=40, ymax=60,
             hatch='\\\\', color='yellow', linewidth=1.5, linecolor='orange', linestyle='--', alpha=0.2,
             zorder=5, label='target band', legend=True)

chart1.vrect(xmin='2020-01', xmax='2021-10',
             hatch='//', color='blue', linewidth=1.5, linecolor='purple', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4,
             zorder=5, label='stable period', legend=True)
```

### Creating with `dict`

Each `hline`, `vline`, `hrect` and `vrect` object is created using a `dict` as input.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_rects_kwargs
#| fig-cap: "Using `dict`"
#| code-fold: show
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns, attrs=attrs,
               title='create with dict',
               xrange='2017-01:', yrange=[0, 120])

# Add hline
kwargs = {'y' : 20, 'xrange' : '2020-03:2025-04',
          'label' : 'horizontal line', 'legend' : True,
          'color' : 'grey', 'linewidth' : 0.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'alpha' : 0.8}
chart1.hline(**kwargs)

# Add vline
kwargs = {'x' : '2020-03', 'yrange' : [30, 60],
          'label' : 'vertical line', 'legend' : True,
          'color' : 'grey', 'linewidth' : 0.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'alpha' : 0.8}
chart1.vline(**kwargs)

# Add hrect
kwargs = {'ymin' : 80, 'ymax' : 100,
          'hatch' : '++',
          'label' : 'horizontal rectangle', 'legend' : True,
          'color' : 'grey', 'linecolor' : 'skyblue', 'linewidth' : 0.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'alpha' : 0.8}
chart1.hrect(**kwargs)

# Add vrect
kwargs = {'xmin' : '2022-01', 'xmax' : '2023-01',
          'hatch' : '////',
          'label' : 'vertical rectangle', 'legend' : True,
          'color' : 'pink', 'linecolor' : 'red', 'linewidth' : 0.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'alpha' : 0.8}
chart1.vrect(**kwargs)
```

### Adding Directly in `Chart()`

Each `hline`, `vline`, `hrect` and `vrect` object is added to `Chart()` using a `list` of `dict`s.

```{python}
#| label: gallery_lines_rects_chart
#| fig-cap: "Adding directly in `Chart()`"
#| code-fold: show

# dicts for each object
kwargs_hline1 = {'y' : 10,
                 'label' : 'low horizontal line', 'legend' : True,
                 'color' : 'grey', 'linewidth' : 1.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'alpha' : 0.7}

kwargs_hline2 = {'y' : 110,
                 'label' : 'high horizontal line', 'legend' : True,
                 'color' : 'blue', 'linewidth' : 1.5, 'linestyle' : '-', 'alpha' : 0.7}

kwargs_vline = {'x' : '2020-03',
                'label' : 'vertical line', 'legend' : True,
                'color' : 'red', 'linewidth' : 0.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'alpha' : 0.7}

kwargs_hrect = {'ymin' : 80, 'ymax' : 100,
                'hatch' : '++',
                'label' : 'horizontal rectangle', 'legend' : True,
                'color' : 'yellow', 'linecolor' : 'red', 'linewidth' : 0.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'alpha' : 0.7}

kwargs_vrect = {'xmin' : '2024-01', 'xmax' : '2024-05',
                'hatch' : '//',
                'label' : 'vertical rectangle', 'legend' : True,
                'color' : 'green', 'linecolor' : 'blue', 'linewidth' : 0.5, 'linestyle' : '--', 'alpha' : 0.7}

# Create `Chart` using kwargs of each object
chart1 = Chart(df, linecols=df.columns, attrs=attrs,
               title='zorder, legend',
               xrange='2017-01:', yrange=[0, 120],
               hlines=[kwargs_hline1, kwargs_hline2],
               vlines=[kwargs_vline],
               hrects=[kwargs_hrect],
               vrects=[kwargs_vrect])
```

:::


## Add Text, Arrows, Fill

Similar to horizontal/vertical lines and shades, text, arrows and fill reigons can be specified.
Below is an example of

```{python}
#| label: text_arrow_fill_data
```
